Let's rock and roll. This is going to be awesome. We're so intrigued by this particular competency vision focus because this really is the base basis of where it all came from for you. Can you tell us about that lightning moment? When was that point in your life? Where were you when you realized how important it was to have this clear vision focus? It's good question, Siya. I think that I was actually using this competency of vision focus unconsciously, meaning without even knowing it, long before I ever thought, hey, I know what I need to do. I need to actually develop a compelling vision for my future and learn to make that my target and then go after that with a laser-guided focus. I didn't realize that I was doing it, but was actually doing it from the time that I was a kid. I started my first business at the age of eight. It was a paper out. It was knocking on doors. And before I ever knocked on my first door, I remember even today visualizing, A, what it was going to be like to knock on the door, running the scenario through my mind before it actually happened. And then imagining how great it would feel in the future, maybe three or four months down the road after I'd knocked on all these doors, had a huge paper out, was making a lot of money, and it was the talk of the town. And my parents were proud of me and I had just sort of like, there's this kid on the go and it felt really, really good. Now, did I run that whole equation through my brain as a child? No, I did it automatically. And that's what some people do. Some people are blessed with just genetically their wire, their sales DNA, their operating system up here is very visually. So, boom, they want something to think about, they visualize it. We hear about it with athletes in the Olympics all the time. They say, you know, he's on the gold, gets the gold medal, he gets interviewed. You know, you know, when did you first think about getting the gold medal? And they'll say, well, I've ran this race 10,000 times in my mind's eye before I ever won. You know, I imagine myself standing on the podium receiving the gold medal before I ever got it. They rat the race was already won before it was ran, right? So that's on an athletic level with someone that's, you know, a world-class athlete. You, and what happens with world-class athletes is you actually get to see the importance of it because their strategy, I mean, their ability to swim, and that's it's a swimmer, right? So he works his tail off to become the best swimmer in the world. And then when you actually see the success manifest as an Olympic gold medal, the guy will say, well, yeah, I worked really hard on the pool, but I also was visualizing like crazy and had this focus on I want to win a gold medal since I was eight years old. So you really see it plain and simple with an Olympic athlete. To that matter, if you actually speak to any really successful businessman and you say, you know, what, what did, how did it start? You know, how'd you become successful? They probably won't say in the beginning, well, I had this vision and I, you know, knew where I wanted to go and so on and so forth. But when you actually break it down for them, you make them slow and they'll say, whoa, go back for a second. Tell me how it started. What were you thinking? And inevitably, invariably, they will all say, well, I had this idea and I am not going to imagine if it would be like this and I saw the world a different way, a better way, whether it's a Steve Jobs, a Warren Buffett, a Bill Gates, doesn't mean, you know, from the most successful businessman in the world to a mom and pop shop to a salesman who's about to embark on a career and knows he wants to be a top producer before they ever start. They always visualize where they're going before they get there. Now here's the deal. There's two ways to go about this. One way would be to say, well, great, you know, I don't have to work on my vision because I know what's happening automatically. The problem with that is then you're leaving it up to chance because some of us really aren't that good at developing compelling visions for our future. We've never been taught how to do it. Others are naturally great at it. So what I do when I train a sales force or an individual person, I take luck or I take, you know, any happenstance out of the equation. I say, wait, whoa, whoa, I can show you exactly how the system essentially for developing an unbelievably compelling vision for your future, one that first inspires you. That's how it starts. That's the first. When you think about your vision, when you see it in your mind's eye, you have to like make sure you want to jump out of bed in the morning. And secondly, you have to learn, and this is where straight line is so effective, to communicate, to essentially share that vision with other people in a way that inspires them and moves them to take action. Remember this on some level, every human being is thirsty for a vision and so few human beings have one. So what do they do? They gravitate towards someone with a compelling vision. People will never follow your goals in life. They follow vision. So for me, it started at a young age, didn't know what was doing it, but somewhere I think around me in my early 20s, when I started to train salespeople actively, I started realizing as I look back in my life and saying, you know, what is it going to take for me to get these other people to succeed? I started to realize that when I spoke to people, very few of them actually knew where they wanted to go. They're like, I want to be rich. I'm like, why? I like money. Why? Because I want a nice car. Why? Because I can get a hot girl if I get a nice car. I'm like, okay, great. And then what? Well, you know, I love to retire. My parents, oh, okay, so your parents struggle. You can see maybe you're taking care of your parents. Yeah, all of a sudden the emotion comes through and they get moved emotionally when they start to think about what they can really do with money, what success really means to them, which means helping other people and being essentially a pillar and a life force in a positive way to all those around you. And that's when you start to inspire people. That's when you start to inspire yourself. And it's the first step essentially in unleashing the power, the personal power that you have that allows you to break through any barrier, any obstacle, anything that's standing in your way. And it's knowing where you want to go. So vividly and saying, you know, I see where I got to go and being willing to do the real world work, meaning learn the things you need to learn, like the straight line system or like you did. You want to be a psychologist. You went to school and got a degree. You know, you could have wanted to be a psychologist, but you want to do the work, you'd never get there. So once you get that initial vision, bam, it's just like that's where it starts. And I don't know rambling here, but it's such important stuff tied up in that vision. And I think that's really the underpinning of everything is something I call your why. You know, why are you doing what you're doing? In other words, people say, you know, I want to make money. Why? Because I want to cut why. And then when you get four or five levels down to the real why, and you can essentially hook someone into their own why, make them aware of really why this is important and why their vision should move them. All of a sudden, wow, then you see someone becoming self-motivated. And I say, you know, Jordan, I'm not motivated. How do I motivate myself? Or, you know, how do I motivate other people? What's your why? If you're not motivated, you don't have a powerful why. That's the beginning of motivation to know why where you want to go is important to you. And where you want to go is what? It's your vision for your future. So it's the vision, the ability to learn to focus on the vision. Those two together, it sets you up for massive success in the real world. All right, you're back. Pretty cool, right? One point I want to highlight here is that I have actually been through this with an Olympic swimmer who really just told me this exact story how before you won his medal, he'd run it through his own mind 10,000 times. And if you really check around online, you'll see this is almost like everyone does this, who's successful. They run the movie out. They see themselves succeeding before they ever get there. It's a crucial point. And along with that, of course, you have to know your why. That's where you're paralyzed.
